title: Dog Chewing Gum Motion With No Food: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog making chewing motions without food? Learn what this behavior means, why it is usually not an emergency, and how a video can help your vet.
content_html:
TL;DR: Gum chewing behavior is usually not an emergency and can be caused by dental pain, nausea, or neurological issues. Record a video of the behavior and schedule a veterinary exam to ensure an accurate diagnosis.

What does it mean when my dog acts like they are chewing gum?

  • Also known as "empty chewing" or "fly-snapping," this involves rhythmic jaw movements despite having no food in the mouth.
  • This behavior can stem from dental discomfort, nausea, or neurological issues such as focal seizures.
  • In many cases, it is a way for a dog to deal with an unusual taste or a physical sensation in their mouth or throat.

Is it an emergency if my dog exhibits gum-chewing behavior?

  • The urgency level for this behavior is generally low if your dog is alert, responsive, and behaving normally otherwise.
  • It becomes a higher priority if the behavior is accompanied by distress, respiratory changes, or an inability to swallow.
  • While not an emergency, it warrants a scheduled veterinary visit to rule out underlying health issues.

Why should I record a video of my dog's chewing behavior for the vet?

  • Episodes can be intermittent and often stop by the time you reach the veterinary clinic.
  • Capturing a clear video is one of the most effective tools for triage and diagnosis.
  • A visual record helps the veterinarian distinguish between physical irritation, a behavioral quirk, or a neurological event.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In dogs, repetitive chewing motions without food ingestion can stem from various underlying causes. Vomiting may be a related sign resulting from dietary indiscretion, parasitic infection, bacterial or viral gastroenteritis, anxiety, or motion sickness. If the chewing behavior is accompanied by persistent vomiting, depression, abdominal discomfort, or fever, reevaluation by a veterinarian is warranted. Diagnostic efforts should include a thorough examination of the oral cavity to rule out foreign material, abdominal palpation, and abdominal radiographs to identify radiopaque foreign objects or signs of intestinal distention indicative of possible obstruction. Endoscopic examination may further assist in identifying foreign bodies or mass lesions. Treatment depends on the underlying cause, but in cases of foreign body ingestion, monitoring via serial abdominal radiographs may be appropriate for small, smooth objects. However, endoscopic or surgical retrieval is generally recommended if the foreign body is not progressing or if signs of obstruction or clinical deterioration are evident.

Chapter: Gastroenterology, Nutrition

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1956)

faqs.question: Could there be something stuck in my dog's teeth?
faqs.answer: Yes, a very common cause for this motion is a foreign object, such as a piece of a stick or a bone fragment, lodged between the teeth or against the roof of the mouth.

faqs.question: Does this behavior mean my dog is having a seizure?
faqs.answer: It can. Focal seizures sometimes manifest as repetitive jaw snapping or chewing motions. A veterinarian will look for other signs to confirm if this is neurological.

faqs.question: Can nausea cause a dog to make chewing motions?
faqs.answer: Absolutely. Dogs often smack their lips or make chewing motions when they feel nauseated or are about to vomit, as they produce excess saliva during these times.
